title: Ep. 10 - Learning Probabilistic Models with Daphne Koller - YouTube
text:
Welcome to Machine Learning, How Did We
Get Here? I'm Tom Mitchell, your podcast
host.
During the 1990s,
one of the key developments in machine
learning was the introduction of
learning of Bayes nets, or sometimes
they were called probabilistic graphical
models. The idea there was to represent
probabilistic knowledge in a way that
had been introduced by Turing Award
winner Judea Pearl.
But his work left open the question, how
would we learn these from data?
One of the key contributors to machine
learning algorithms for learning Bayes
nets and for working with probabilistic
methods more generally
is Daphne Koller, our guest today.
Daphne is an elected member of the US
National Academy of Engineering and of
the American Academy of Arts and
Sciences. I hope you enjoy the
conversation with Daphne.
>> [music]
[music]
>> Today I'm happy to have with me Daphne
Koller, who is one of the pioneers in
machine learning. Daphne, good to have
you here. Great to be here, Tom. Thank
you. Now, you've done a lot of work in
different aspects of machine learning,
but you're well known on and you wrote a
textbook on uh probabilistic graphic
models.
Graphical models. So, um let me start by
just asking, can you describe
probabilistic graphical models in a
single sentence?
Oof. Probabilistic graphical models are
a mathematical framework that allows you
to model the interrelationships and
specifically importantly causal
relationships between different
variables
in a complex domain.
And then be able to ask questions about
some while observing others in a way
that respects the
uh rules of causality.
That's great. Now you can add as many
sentences as you like. And uh let me
just ask, how did you
personally get into this field? What was
your background that you could have done
a hundred thousand things. How did you
end up doing this?
So, throughout my life I've had an
interest in complexity and putting order
and getting order out of chaos and um
coming up with mathematical frameworks
that allow you to think about complex
phenomena in a rigorous systematic way
that isn't just gut intuition. So, I
started out my career in
uh
actually thinking about game theory and
look thinking about the interactions
between multiple agents and how they can
rationally make decisions in that
context. And having worked on that for a
while, I came to the conclusion that
actually there's a lot of complexity
and open questions even in just a single
agent having to make decisions in a
complex varying environment and we
needed to think about doing that before
we left all the way into the multi-agent
scenario. And so I kind of ratcheted
back almost and said, "Okay, fine. I'm
going to focus first on multi-agent
systems. Sorry, sorry, I'm going to
focus first on single-agent systems."
And then spent a little bit of time
saying, "Okay, well, how do I model a
complex environment so that agents can
make smart decisions?" And that's where
I got into probabilistic graphical
models to begin with. And came very
quickly to the realization that the big
bottleneck was getting people to write
down world knowledge in a way that that
actually reflected the complexities and
subtleties of the world. And so this
task that used to be called knowledge
engineering was really painful, onerous,
and frankly impossible for people to
execute well, which led me naturally to
the path of, "Well, let's just learn it
from data." And so I came into machine
learning
through reasoning um and needing to come
up with a better way of getting models
of complex environments.
And so when people today ask me, "How
did you start doing AI?" my response is
that I didn't ever officially quote
start doing AI. I started doing a thing
that was at that point not considered AI
at all. I mean, we were at best fringe
AI at the time that I started doing it
and excluded um from AI in the minds of
many then practitioners of the of what
was called AI at the time. It's more
that AI became the thing that I was
doing.
That's great. So, when was this, roughly
time, what year was?
So, I started working on probabilistic
models around the early '90s, so '91,
'92. Um and by, you know, '93, '4, I was
kind of like full-fledged into the
machine learning side of that work. Uh
and then
as I went into Stanford um as a faculty
member in 1995,
I became, I would say, a more
broad-based machine learning person in
terms of the work that I was doing
beyond just the, you know, probabilistic
graphical models. I also worked, as you
may remember, Tom, on active learning
and learning and transfer learning and
multitask learning and things that are
now considered sort of the norm of how
we do machine learning. But back in the
day, if you um we all remember, those of
us who were around in those early days,
machine learning was viewed very much as
a very simple linear task of you have
your input, you have your one target
output, and you just want to eke out as
much performance as you can from solving
that one simple supervised learning
problem. And God forbid, you know,
active learning, transfer learning,
multitask was all sort of considered
very much, I would say,
outside the scope of normal machine
learning work at the time.
So, you got involved right at the very
beginning of the work on
uh graphical models. Pearl had
just published his book, I guess. Yeah.
>> Um
so who were the
people you interacted with in that
subfield? What I mean, you know, did you
have people who were
um what you know, inspirational people
to you or mentors or
was it pretty much you were
coming in this from a different angle?
Well, I mean, Judea Pearl obviously is
one of the most impressive and and
forward-thinking people that uh that
I've had the fortune to know. And so he
was clearly a a bit of a role model on
how do we think rigorously about um
about questions like this.
There were not very many of us at the
time. Um several of us are still around.
So, Eric Horvitz, of course, I still
hang out with him. He's the chief
scientist of Microsoft and has been
super influential in uh building some of
those ideas into Microsoft and also
leading a lot of uh Microsoft sort of
interest in AI more broadly. I mean,
Stuart Russell, my postdoc advisor, was
uh also very much a kind of a an early
leader in this space and and an advocate
for
the inclusion of, you know, more
rigorous uh
systematic ways of thinking about
uncertainty and decision-making and
rationality
that were very foreign to this to the uh
field at the time. And Stuart's textbook
on AI, I think, was the first time
someone laid a stake in the ground that
said, "This is a different way to think
about AI that goes beyond logic-based
systems and their improving and expert
systems." Which was, I know, seems so
remote when we think about modern-day
AI, where no one even barely remembers
those days. But that was the norm. I
mean, in 1985, which was before I
entered the before I even started my
PhD, but there was this uh
but Judea told me the story, there was
this panel at IJCAI, which is the big AI
conference. Um and this was the one of
the previous AI bubbles that
unfortunately got punctured just as I
started my PhD. But uh because it was
based all around, you know, logic-based
systems and expert systems that were
never going to scale. But the panel was
uh on the topic of, "Do numbers have a
role to play in artificial
intelligence?" And the consensus of that
panel, with the sole exception of Judea
Pearl, was no. People do not use numbers
in how they think about the world and
therefore our AI systems should not use
numbers either. It was And when you
think that today, where there is really
no other AI other than based on numbers.
And by the way, also, if you think about
neuroscience and how neuro, you know,
like how human intelligence actually
works. Um it's that seems ridiculous
when you think about the study. But that
was the consensus of the panel back in
1985, which maybe explains why that
particular bubble burst and it took a
while before things recovered. I, you
know, when I graduated with my PhD from
Stanford in 1993,
you couldn't actually say you were doing
AI. You had to say you were doing
cognitive computing or statistical
learning theory or some other thing that
was not constraint satisfaction. You
couldn't say you were doing AI because
that was considered a fringe discipline
at best.
I want I want to get your picture of how
the field evolved
since you were involved.
But let me start by asking specifically
about graphical models. So you were
right in the midst of that. How did that
feel look like kind of the key steps?
How do you see the evolution of that
subfield of machine learning?
So probabilistic graphical models for me
emerged
by realizing that the
problems that we needed to solve to
address most real-world applications
went beyond you have a
vector representation of an input and a
single often times binary or at best
continuous output. We um
there was so much more opportunity to
think about richly structured
environments that and and richly
structured problems. So even if you
think about
the
you know, problems like
understanding what is in an image.
That's not a single label problem of
there is a dog because images are
complex and there is interrelationships
between the different objects in them
and and you wanted to get beyond the yes
no is there a dog in this image to
something that is much more rich of you
know, there's a dog and a frisbee and a
beach and three kids building a sand
castle. And so you have a rich input and
a rich output. And so thinking about
these richly structured domains
gave rise to well, we have to think
about multiple variables. We have to
think about the interactions between
those variables and leverage that
structure both in our input and our
output
space in order to get to much much
better conclusions and deal with the
problems that really matter.
Right. And so
um
the field kind of evolved um
from
maybe first having this notion of we can
represent these joint probability
distributions over these multiple
components
with a some kind of graphical
representation. But then the question of
how to do inference, how to do learning
on that. There were some theoretical
developments about tractability,
learnability. There were um algorithmic
developments about uh tractable
approximations for inference. If If you
think about
where we are today in terms of our
understanding
of how to
uh build such systems compared to when
we began, what what are the kind of key
developments that you saw or see
um over the years
in
as that evolved?
So I think that the the work that was
done on probabilistic graphical models
ended up having multiple quite diverse
progeny in terms of impact that it had
on the machine learning space as a
whole. First of all, the techniques that
were Well, first of all, it was the
first time that anyone had even
thought about learning a probability
distribution of the world versus things
that were more straightforward class
classification problems. So trying to
actually capture the structure in the
domain. So that was something that is
very much uh at the heart of today's,
for example, generative models where
they basically generate via processes
like autoencoders and so on, they they
actually generate distributions.
Or they use a distributional model to
generate, for example, the the the text
that we see in in the large language
models. And so the the conceptual
framework as well as some even
you algorithmic ideas like variational
approximations
and so on really fed into the
development of some of the generative
models that that we see today. So that's
kind of one
progeny, if you will. I think the notion
of complex structured outputs and
multi-task
learning and multi-task classification
also a lot of it has its roots in some
of the probabilistic graphical models
developments that happened. And then
finally, and maybe less prominently
today, but I think absolutely critical
as we think about the next stage of
development is coming back to this
notion of causality versus correlation
and thinking about the differences
between them and the role that each of
them has to play. So for example, in the
work that I'm doing right now at
Insitro, which
deals with using machine learning and
probability for answering what is a
fundamentally causal question of if I
make an intervention in a human being by
modulating a particular target, will it
make this person better? What will it
do? Will it
will it help treat the disease that they
have? That is fundamentally a causal
question and you can't answer that with
correlational data without falling prey
to all sorts of, you know, really false
and misleading conclusions. And so a lot
of the tools that were developed as part
of causal inference within the framework
of probabilistic graphical models are
now utilized within
work that we and others are doing to
answer questions about causality.
Right now in the broader AI universe,
there's less of an emphasis on answering
causal questions because there's just so
much to be done on
basically
purely observational questions. Like
here's a bunch of whatever documents
that I want to summarize and distill.
There's no causality and that that that
is purely an inferential question in the
context of the, you know,
patterns that you see you can see in the
text. But as we start taking some of
those incredibly powerful developments
in in, you know, generative AI and large
language models and
putting it to work in actual agentic
systems that intervene in the real
world, causality becomes important again
and thinking about the difference
between correlation and causation is
going to become increasingly important
as the complexity of the tasks that we
undertake with agentic AI and even more
so as agentic AI makes its way out of
the realm of purely virtual and into the
physical world where consequences
could be
much much more, fun, you know, important
and often times even,
um
matters of life and death and
self-driving cars or drug development,
you need to start thinking really hard
about the difference between correlation
and causation. Yeah, that's a really
good point. Um in fact, you know,
you're one of the people who has
lived um both an academic
career
um
developing very fundamental theory and
approach algorithms for
these kinds of techniques and then moved
to the commercial world to try to apply
them. And as you say, um when you move
to the commercial world, then certain
issues become even more important and
kind of draw your attention. Like in
this case, you're mentioning causality.
Um say more about your experience
uh trying commercialize
uh
this kind of
um AI, this kind of learning.
Um
what were some of the biggest surprises,
the biggest challenges
as you decided to
um move out into the commercial world?
So first of all, I wouldn't characterize
at least my own motivations in
undertaking this journey as commercial,
but rather as applied and wanting to see
the conceptual work that I'd been doing,
the theoretical work that I've been
doing actually make a difference in the
real world. And I will tell you that I
tried earlier to do that within
academia. So for me, one of those um
kind of fundamental epiphany moments
came when I worked on
what I still consider to be some very
exciting work on
understanding tumor biopsies using
computer vision and using that to reach
conclusions about
what a patient's prognosis might be and
maybe their response to different
treatments and so on and so forth. And I
worked on that at Stanford as an
academic. And when we saw that even back
then, and this is pre-deep learning,
this was 2010 or so,
uh
the results that we were getting were
superior to that of your average
pathologist and equivalent at the time
to even the best of the best
pathologists. And I was like, wow, this
is really impactful. It could make a
difference to patients. And we walked we
went around from one whatever device
manufacturer,
hospitals and said, "Please adopt this
technology. It's so valuable. It could
make a difference to your patients.
Please use this." I wasn't trying to
make money. I was just trying to get
this to be useful to patients. And
they're like, "Do you have a product?" I
was like, "I don't have a product.
Here's a paper. You can look at the
paper and you can tell you what to do."
And then I don't we don't do papers. We
need a product. Do you have a product?
Come back and we'll consider whether
we're going to buy the product. And I
very quickly came to the realization
that if you want to have a real impact,
you need to build something that people
would want to use. It's not enough to
write papers and hope that someone finds
them sufficiently interesting to go on
and and you know, build the thing that
would make them useful for themselves.
And so that was
to me at the time
like the realization that I wanted
to see the work that I was doing make a
difference in the world. And in order to
do that, that one had to be at a company
because the incentives are just not
aligned to do that within academia. You
don't build products in academia. It's
not in the incentive of any one of your
grad students or postdocs to build a
product. Um they need to get the data
that they
um that they need for, you know, the
figures in their paper. And when their
paper is done, they go off and they get
their whatever next academic job. And
building a product that has persistence,
that has all the nitty-gritty kind of
like, you know, data pipelines, uh
reusability,
it's just not anybody's incentive
function. And not to mention the NSF or
the NIH is not going to pay for that.
So,
um you had to do it in a environment
where that was the incentivized
behavior. And so that's what led me out
of academia
uh to go build things in the real world.
And I think you find that
there are some very big differences.
First of all, the focus of the work is
not do you use the coolest, most elegant
machine learning method, it's do you use
the thing that works. Um and so you need
to prioritize that. And sometimes that
involves doing
fancy, really cutting-edge things. And
sometimes it's just XGBoost just works
and it's hard to beat. And maybe you
can't write a paper about that, but it's
the thing that can get you to to make a
a thing that actually works in the real
world. as in the fastest possible way.
So, you have to balance where you invest
your energy in building the coolest,
most exciting stuff and where you just
do the stuff that works. I also found
that the
need to solve a real problem gives you a
laser focus that allows you to strip
away a whole bunch of stuff that doesn't
matter and focus on the stuff that does
matter. And I think that's a very
clarifying lens
uh relative to being an academic where
like, "Oh, this is cool. And this is
also cool. This is also How do I
prioritize being I don't know, they're
all cool." But if you have a lens of I
need to build this thing, it really
helps provide a lot of clarity. And then
I would say the last thing, which is
maybe the most rewarding, is that
the
corporate world incentivizes
teamwork in ways that academia does not
because in academia at the end of the
day, you're either the first or last
author on the paper or you're somewhere
in the middle. And so each person has to
own
their thing. Even the words PI,
principal investigator,
almost by
define that there needs to be like a,
you know, a single person who's like at
the head of the helm of the ship, right?
Um whereas in the
in the world of you're building
something and you succeed or fail as a
team, it's kind of built into the
into the framework. You're building a
company,
the company either succeeds in the sense
that it has a product that people want
and pay money for and and provides
value,
or you fail as a team. And
incentivizing your own self-interest is
not going to get you very far. Now, I
think as you get into larger companies
where there's factions and, you know, I
need to be successful at the expense of
somebody sitting in the office next
door, it might become more complex.
Fortunately, I've not been in that
environment, so I can't speak to that.
But in the startup environment, which is
where I've spent, you know, all of my
work,
it's really about you you succeed or
fail as a team.
And that's really great. It's just so
liberating to have people that you know
have your back because we all have
the same goal.
That's that's that's great.
Let's see. Before we run out of time, I
want to get your
picture of the field
of machine learning um
not only how it evolved, you've spoken
about that a little, but where you think
we are today. And um
uh what are some of the
surprises to you, if any, about where we
are um when you got into this in the
late '80s, early '90s, you had some
picture, no doubt, or some answer when
people would ask you, "Where's this all
going to lead us?" You know, I think
that uh
I I would not have I would not have said
that I could have predicted back in the
day that we would end up in the I mean,
certainly not in the early '90s that we
would end up with
artificial intelligence as powerful as
it is today. I would say that to my mind
that realization came
around 2012, 2013, 14, and so on, when
you really started to see the
exponential
curve manifesting in front of your eyes,
where the performance of systems on
problems that,
you know, back in the, you know,
late '90s, early 2000s, you would have
said, "Oh, that's a 50-year goal that we
might get to to, you know,
whatever, this level of image
recognition, this level of text
understanding." And you look at them and
year after year the performance just
like goes like this.
I don't think
I would not have predicted that back in,
you know, when I got into this. It's
been incredible to see it happening, so
rewarding, so exciting.
I think exponential curves have
are very tricky things. It's very
difficult to estimate the base of the
exponent, and slight shifts in the base
of the exponent can give you radically
different um outcomes in in a
5-to-10-year timeframe even. And so the
progress has been, I would say, way
faster than I would have expected.
And but I would say that and maybe the
other surprise and yet not a surprise
has been
the incredible
impact of just having
so much data at your disposal. When we
started in this space, and I'm sure you
remember this, Tom, large data sets were
a couple hundred samples. You were like,
"Wow, I have a large data set. It has a
couple hundred. I have a thousand.
That's amazing." Right?
Um
and now we're dealing with data at a
scale that none of us could ever have
envisioned as ever becoming available to
us.
And I think
really kind of
highlighting Rich Sutton's bitter
lesson, which is
it's all about the data. It's just all
about the data. And there's things that
you just can't even begin to do until
your data crosses a certain threshold.
And when you do,
magic begins to happen. All these
emergent properties that we're seeing
today happens when you have models that
hit a certain level of capacity by
virtue of having
that much data to train on.
And I think that to me was actually part
of the realization that led me to found
In Silico back in 2018 with the
understanding that in biology and
medicine, we have not hit web-scale
data.
Um and so we are we were were at the
time fundamentally at in the regime
where, you know, maybe not quite as
small scale of a data as when you and I
started, but thousands of samples, maybe
a couple hundred thousand samples, but
not millions and tens of millions and
hundreds of millions of samples, which
is why we built the data printing
factory that we have right now, which is
how do we create at scale data? Maybe
not web-scale data, but at scale data to
drive our understanding of fundamental
biology of in our case human health and
disease.
And I think that's a lesson that
um we should keep we should be mindful
of because a lot of people don't still
don't understand that
data is not all data is data is not like
fungible. The fact that we have
web-scale data for text and images of
cats and dogs and airplanes
doesn't mean that we can just take that
and transfer those capabilities like
that to asking answering questions about
human biology where the data that we
need has never even been generated. So,
you need to have the right kind of data
in order to get to that sort of
exponential curve and capabilities.
Could you give us a glimpse of the
diversity and maybe volume of data that
you're working with at In Silico? Yeah.
So, we really try and bridge um
biological data across multiple scales.
And so we have data from cells and data
from humans. Uh data from cells is
fortunately uh
I don't want to say effectively
unlimited, but can be generated at
fairly massive scales. And we've made a
huge investment in building a cell
factory that generates different types
of cellular systems from human derived
cells, perturbs them to answer the
causality question. So, if I intervene
at this gene, what changes about cell
state across multiple different
modalities of measuring the cells
because if you look at cells from
different perspectives, often different
phenomena reveal themselves. So, we
measure molecular data, omics, also
measure imaging data which often gives
you a very different perspective. And we
can print, you know,
100 million cells in with multiple
different perturbations in a single
experiment. And we have robots that do
that. And by having robots do that, not
only is it faster and more efficient,
it's also much, much higher quality data
than when people pipette by hand where
you have inevitable batch effects and
artifacts of you hold the pipette in a
slightly different angle, you get a
different result.
Um, so that's one type of data. But our
fundamental realization at the get-go
was that
you can study cells all day long, but
people are not individual cells. People
have a lot of complexity of cells and
and organs and interplay between organs
and how that gives rise to system-level
phenomena of a human. And so, we also
bring together bring together into this
factory a whole bunch of human data
measuring human biology at multiple
scales. Omics in blood and tissue,
organ-level imaging via MRI and DEXA
scans and CAT scans and so on. And then
system-level phenomena like, you know,
what kind of disease do you have? And do
you have fibrosis and so on and so
forth. And really figuring out how AI
can both interrogate each of those
individual data modalities at their
relevant scale, but also then synthesize
together into this thing that we're
calling the virtual human so that we can
start to answer questions about if I
make an intervention in a human being,
is it going to what what consequences
beneficial or adverse is it going to
have?
So, that story, which is very
interesting,
um,
reminds me of a question that some
people have been asking me lately, which
is um, I'm thinking of going to
graduate school to get a PhD in machine
learning. Should I really do that? Or
should I go instead to a company that
has
the kind of resources needed to create
large enough data sets and to have
enough computational resources to really
make a difference. Which of those should
I do?
Um,
I'm sure people have been asking you
that question too often.
Um,
if you could give advice to such a
person, what's your advice?
My answer would be it really depends on
what you want to do. So, in two ways.
First of all, some people just want to
be academics with all of the insolent
academic freedom and being PI of your
own lab. If you want to be an academic,
you can't do that without a PhD. That's
just the way the system works.
Whether that's right or wrong, doesn't
matter. It is what it is. And if you
want to be a professor, you need to have
a PhD. And I think it's not an
unreasonable thing to have to go through
the process of being a trainee before
you go and train somebody else. So, I'm
not even saying it's the wrong thing.
Uh, I think if you want to do really
deep
theoretical work,
uh, it's very hard to find space for
that within a company. I mean, some of
the larger research labs used to carve
out more space for people to do purely
conceptual work. I think with the
arms race going on in AI right now,
there's less patience for that um, and
more focus on like, okay, we need to
ship AI products. And so, I think maybe
there's less room for that in industry
even in the larger companies perhaps.
But
um, but if you want to do something that
is actually applied and has real data
and frankly enabled by real compute and
oftentimes requires that team
um, collaboration that is so important
for larger projects, you're going to
probably be better off doing that in
industry versus in academia. So, I would
say the subset of
people for whom academia is the right
choice
as a proportion
is probably smaller. As a raw number,
it's still really large because, you
know, not everybody wants to do AI and
machine learning. So, I know that
there's no dearth of applicants for
academic institutions with an AI
expertise. But I think as a proportion
on the individual level,
if what you want is to build
something that actually works in the
real world, you're probably better off
uh, at least off, you know,
you're probably better off doing that in
an industry setting.
You know, one of the angles on this
um,
as you know, um, AI plus X, AI plus
different sciences, uh, the intersection
of AI and pretty much any field you can
